name: Rock of Cashel
description: A spectacular cluster of medieval buildings set on a limestone outcrop in County Tipperary. The complex includes a round tower, Romanesque chapel, Gothic cathedral, and 15th-century castle.
history: The Rock of Cashel, also known as Cashel of the Kings and St. Patrick's Rock, is a historical site located dramatically above a plain at Cashel, County Tipperary, Ireland.

name: Cahir Castle
description: One of Ireland's largest and best-preserved castles, Cahir sits on a rocky island in the River Suir. Built by the Butler family, it withstood sieges until Cromwell's artillery breached its walls in 1650.
history: Cahir Castle, one of the largest castles in Ireland, is sited on an island in the river Suir. It was built from 1142 by Conchobar Ua Briain, King of Thomond. Now situated in Cahir town centre, County Tipperary, the castle is well preserved and has guided tour and audiovisual shows in multiple languages.

name: Clonmacnoise
description: One of Ireland's most important early monastic sites, founded by St Ciarán in 544 on the banks of the Shannon. The complex includes cathedral ruins, round towers, high crosses, and numerous church remains.
history: Clonmacnoise or Clonmacnois is a ruined monastery in County Offaly in Ireland on the River Shannon south of Athlone, founded in 544 by Saint Ciarán, from Rathcroghan, County Roscommon. Until the 9th century it had close associations with the kings of Connacht.

name: Roscrea Castle
description: A 13th-century Norman castle with a gate tower and curtain walls enclosing an 18th-century Damer House. The keep and surrounding walls provide an excellent example of Anglo-Norman military architecture.
history: Roscrea Castle is a 13th-century motte-and-bailey castle in the town of Roscrea, Ireland. The castle consists of a walled courtyard, gate block, and angled towers. Along with 18th century Damer House and gardens, the castle forms part of Roscrea Heritage Centre.

name: Nenagh Castle
description: The circular keep of Nenagh Castle is one of the finest of its kind in Ireland, standing nearly 30 metres tall. Built by Theobald Walter, ancestor of the Butler dynasty, around 1200.
history: Nenagh is the county town of County Tipperary in Ireland. Nenagh used to be a market town, and the site of the East Munster Ormond Fair.

name: Lea Castle
description: A large Anglo-Norman castle ruin on the banks of the River Barrow near Portarlington. Its impressive keep and gatehouse survive despite centuries of conflict and neglect.
history: Lea Castle is a ruined medieval castle near Portarlington, County Laois. The remains of the castle mostly date to the 13th century and consist of a four-storey donjon and a gatehouse.

name: Dunamase Castle
description: The Rock of Dunamase rises dramatically from the Laois plains, fortified since the 9th century. Strongbow received it as a dowry and the Anglo-Normans built the impressive castle ruin visible today.
history: Dunamase or the Rock of Dunamase is a rocky outcrop in County Laois, Ireland. Rising 46 metres (151 ft) above a plain, it has the ruins of Dunamase Castle, a defensive stronghold dating from the early Hiberno-Norman period with a view across to the Slieve Bloom Mountains. It is near the N80 road between the towns of Portlaoise and Stradbally.

name: Birr Castle
description: Home of the Parsons family since 1620, Birr Castle is famous for its historic science centre and Great Telescope. Its 120-acre demesne gardens hold over 1,000 species of trees and shrubs.
history: Birr Castle is a castle in the town of Birr in County Offaly, Ireland. It is the home of the 7th Earl of Rosse and his family, and as such the castle is generally not open to the public, though the grounds and gardens of the demesne are publicly accessible, and include a science museum and a café, a reflecting telescope which was the largest in the world for decades and a modern radio telescope.

name: Clonony Castle
description: A restored tower house in the midlands said to hold the graves of two cousins of Anne Boleyn. The MacCoghlan stronghold features a fine bawn wall and corner towers.
history: Clonony is a small village in County Offaly, Ireland. It is noted for Clonony Castle, a late medieval tower house built in 1500. The village is between the River Brosna and the Grand Canal, in the parish of Gallen and Reynegh. The nearest town is Cloghan, 2.8 km (1.7 mi) to the south-east along the R357 road. Athlone is 22 km (14 mi) to the north.

name: Charleville Castle
description: A Gothic Revival castle designed by Francis Johnston in 1798, surrounded by ancient oak woods. Considered one of the finest Gothic castles in Ireland, it features elaborate plasterwork and a remarkable gallery.
history: Charleville Castle is a Gothic-style castle located in County Offaly, Ireland, bordering the town of Tullamore, near the River Clodiagh. It is considered one of the finest of its type in the country.

name: Fore Abbey
description: A monastic complex associated with the 'Seven Wonders of Fore', including a church founded by St Féchín. The medieval priory, town gate, and anchorite tower survive in a secluded valley.
history: Fore Abbey is the ruins of a Benedictine and Early Gaelic 7th century Abbey with associated Mill, Anchorite's Cell, Holy Wells and a structure associated with St. Féichín, all situated to the north of Lough Lene in County Westmeath, adjacent to Fore village. Architectural additions, damage by fire and dismantlement have altered the site's appearance and layout over the centuries.

name: Athlone Castle
description: A strategically vital castle guarding the Shannon crossing, besieged during the Jacobite wars. Now a modern museum telling the story of Athlone and the midlands from prehistory to the present.
history: Athlone Castle, sometimes known as Adamson Castle, is a castle located in Athlone, County Westmeath, Ireland, dating from the 12th century.

name: Tullynally Castle
description: The largest castellated house in Ireland, home to the Pakenham family since 1655. Its 30-acre gardens include a Tibetan garden, Chinese garden, and forest walks around Lough Derravaragh.
history: Tullynally Castle, also known as Pakenham Hall, is a country house situated some 2 km from Castlepollard on the Coole village road in County Westmeath, Ireland. The Gothic-style building has over 120 rooms and has been home to the Pakenham family for over 350 years.

name: Killahara Castle
description: A formidable Butler tower house standing proudly in the Tipperary farmland, one of the finest examples of a medieval Irish fortified residence.
history: Built in the 15th century by the Butler family, Killahara Castle is a well-preserved tower house near Dundrum in County Tipperary. The castle features four storeys with vaulted ceilings and a fine bawn wall. It has been carefully restored by its current owners and offers a remarkable glimpse into medieval Irish castle life.

name: Loughmoe Castle
description: An extraordinary hybrid of medieval tower and Elizabethan mansion in the Tipperary countryside, where two ages of architecture collide in one dramatic ruin.
history: Loughmoe Castle combines a 13th-century tower house with a grand 17th-century fortified house added by the Purcell family. The Purcells, Barons of Loughmoe, were one of Tipperary's most powerful Old English families. The building fell into disuse after the Cromwellian confiscations and now stands as a striking ruin showing the evolution of Irish castle architecture.

name: Ballindoney Castle
description: A secluded tower house ruin in the rolling Cork countryside, another link in the chain of medieval fortifications that once controlled the Munster landscape.
history: Ballindoney Castle is a medieval tower house in County Cork, one of many such structures built by local landholding families during the turbulent 15th and 16th centuries. The tower stands in rural farmland and, while ruinous, retains enough structure to convey the scale of medieval fortified domestic architecture in Munster.

name: Ardfinnan Castle
description: A commanding Norman castle on a high rock above the River Suir, guarding a strategic crossing point in the golden vale of Tipperary since the 13th century.
history: Built by King John around 1185 on a dramatic rock above the River Suir, Ardfinnan Castle was one of the earliest Norman fortifications in Tipperary. It controlled a vital river crossing on the route between Waterford and Cashel. The castle passed through various families including the Butlers and was rebuilt in the 19th century. It remains in private hands on its spectacular riverside perch.

name: Ballynahow Round Castle
description: A rare and remarkable circular castle in Tipperary — one of only a handful of round tower houses in Ireland, its unusual form both beautiful and mysterious.
history: Ballynahow Castle near Thurles is one of Ireland's rare circular castle towers, dating to the 16th century. The round design is exceptionally unusual for an Irish tower house. The castle features a spiral staircase within its walls and retains much of its original height. Its unique form has made it a subject of considerable archaeological interest.

name: Ormond Castle
description: Ireland's finest Elizabethan manor house, this remarkable Carrick-on-Suir treasure was built to welcome a queen who never came.
history: Built in the 1560s by 'Black Tom' Butler, 10th Earl of Ormond, Ormond Castle in Carrick-on-Suir is the finest surviving Elizabethan manor house in Ireland. Tom Butler, a cousin and favourite of Elizabeth I, built the elegant unfortified manor to welcome the queen on a planned visit that never materialised. The interior features remarkable decorative plasterwork with portraits of the queen. The manor adjoins two earlier 15th-century tower houses.

name: Terryglass Castle
description: A quiet tower house ruin on the shores of Lough Derg — where the Butler family watched boats drift past and Tipperary's green fields rolled endlessly south.
history: A tower house near the village of Terryglass on the shores of Lough Derg, County Tipperary. Built by the Butler family in the medieval period. The village is also home to the site of a famous early Christian monastery. Located in a scenic lakeside setting.

name: Lackeen Castle
description: A tall, imposing O'Kennedy tower house in north Tipperary, its six storeys of dark stone exuding medieval menace across the boggy lowlands.
history: Built by the O'Kennedy clan in the 15th century, Lackeen Castle is a substantial six-storey tower house near Lorrha in north Tipperary. The O'Kennedys were one of the chief Dalcassian families of north Munster. The castle features a fine bawn wall and retains much of its height and original fabric, including murder holes and a vaulted ground floor.

name: Redwood Castle
description: A beautifully restored Norman tower house in the Tipperary boglands, brought back to life as a centre for clan heritage and medieval culture.
history: Built by the Norman de Marisco family in the 12th century and later held by the MacEgan clan (hereditary Brehon lawyers), Redwood Castle near Lorrha has been carefully restored. The MacEgans ran a famous law school here. The castle now serves as a heritage centre celebrating Gaelic culture and Brehon law, offering medieval banquets and cultural events.

name: Castlepollard Castle
description: A quiet market town's medieval tower, now absorbed into the streetscape of this pretty Westmeath village near the shores of Lough Derravaragh.
history: Castlepollard in County Westmeath takes its name from its medieval castle, associated with the Pollard and later Pakenham families. The town sits near the legendary Lough Derravaragh, associated with the tale of the Children of Lir. The Pakenham family (Earls of Longford) built nearby Tullynally Castle, one of Ireland's largest castellated houses.

name: Delvin Castle
description: A Victorian baronial fantasy with four round towers — one of the last of its kind in Ireland, built on the site of a 12th-century Norman stronghold.
history: The original castle was built in 1181 by Hugh de Lacy for Sir Gilbert de Nugent, who was granted the title Baron of Delvin. A second castle was built in 1639 by the 1st Earl of Westmeath, who burned it himself as Cromwell approached. The present Victorian castle-like building was constructed nearby with cut limestone and four tall corner towers.

name: Fore Castle
description: A Norman motte and fortification in the extraordinary monastic village of Fore, home to the famous 'Seven Wonders' of Irish folklore.
history: Fore in County Westmeath is one of Ireland's most remarkable heritage sites, combining a Norman motte castle with an extensive Benedictine priory founded in the 7th century. The 'Seven Wonders of Fore' include a monastery built on a bog, water that flows uphill, and a stone raised by prayer. The castle motte dates to the Norman period and overlooks the entire valley settlement.

name: Rathconnell Tower
description: A modest medieval tower in the Westmeath midlands, a quiet survivor from the days when the Anglo-Norman frontier pushed west into Gaelic Ireland.
history: Rathconnell Tower is a medieval fortification in County Westmeath, dating to the period of Anglo-Norman expansion into the Irish midlands. The tower served as a local stronghold and reflects the pattern of Norman settlement in the region. The surrounding area contains numerous other medieval and pre-Norman archaeological sites.

name: Leap Castle
description: The world's most haunted castle — a blood-soaked O'Carroll stronghold in the Offaly countryside where a 'Bloody Chapel' conceals an oubliette stuffed with human remains.
history: Built in the early 1500s by the warring O'Carroll clan of Éile. Infamous for the 'Bloody Chapel' where one O'Carroll brother murdered another during Mass. An oubliette was discovered containing hundreds of skeletons. Burned by IRA forces in 1922 during the Irish Civil War. Being lovingly restored by current owner Sean Ryan. Reputedly haunted by an elemental entity and multiple ghosts.

name: Cloghan Castle
description: A sturdy Offaly tower house rising from ancient monastic foundations — where a 6th-century saint's monastery became a Norman stronghold on the banks of the Shannon.
history: Originally a monastery founded by St Crónán around 600 AD. The Normans fortified the site, and the current tower house dates from the medieval period. Located in the parish of Lusmagh, County Offaly, near Banagher on the River Shannon.

name: Ballycowan Castle
description: A striking Offaly tower house with an unusual rounded turret — built by the O'Molloys who lorded over the midland bogs for centuries.
history: A tower house in County Offaly built by the O'Molloy family, who were chieftains of Fercal. Features a distinctive rounded turret. The castle dates from the late medieval period and passed through various hands after the Cromwellian conquests.

name: Aghaboe Abbey
description: An ancient monastic site in the heart of Ireland where saints and scholars walked — layers of history from the 6th century to the Reformation lie beneath quiet ruins.
history: A monastic site in County Laois founded by St Canice in the 6th century, who also founded the cathedral in Kilkenny. It became a Dominican priory in the 13th century. The ruins include parts of the medieval church and a 15th-century tower. One of Ireland's most important early Christian sites.

name: Ballaghmore Castle
description: A sturdy 15th-century tower house standing sentinel on the ancient road to Munster — built by an Irish chieftain and scarred by Cromwell's cannons.
history: Built in 1480 by Mac Giolla Phádraig (Fitzpatrick), an Irish chieftain, on the Bealach Mor, the ancient road to Munster. Like other castles of its era, it was damaged by Cromwellian forces. Located in County Laois near Roscrea.

name: Morett Castle
description: A ruined tower house in the Laois flatlands, a remnant of the O'More clan's fierce resistance against Tudor plantation.
history: Morett Castle in County Laois dates to the medieval period and was associated with the O'More (O'Moore) clan, who vigorously resisted the Tudor plantation of Laois (then called Queen's County). The area was the scene of some of the fiercest resistance to English colonisation in the 16th century. The castle ruins reflect this turbulent frontier history.

name: Granard Motte
description: One of Ireland's highest and most ancient Norman mottes — a colossal earthwork towering over County Longford, crowned with a statue of St Patrick and views that stretch forever.
history: A massive motte built by the Normans around 1199 on the site of an earlier Celtic settlement, with a traceable history to 236 CE. At 161 metres above sea level, it dominates the town of Granard in County Longford. Topped by a statue of St Patrick erected in 1932. One of the largest mottes in Ireland.

name: Rathcline Castle
description: Remnants of an O'Farrell stronghold in the Longford lakelands, guarding the crossing point where ancient routes met the Shannon.
history: Rathcline Castle near Lanesborough, County Longford, was a stronghold of the O'Farrell clan on the shores of Lough Ree. The castle controlled a strategic crossing of the Shannon and was involved in various conflicts. The O'Farrells were the dominant Gaelic clan of Longford. The castle site overlooks the Shannon at one of its widest points.

name: Athassel Priory
description: Ireland's largest medieval priory, sprawling across four acres of Tipperary farmland — burned twice in its turbulent history, its Gothic arches still frame the sky above the River Suir.
history: The largest medieval priory in Ireland, stretching over 4 acres. Founded in the late 12th century by William de Burgh (founder of the Burke dynasty) for the Augustinians. Burned in 1329 by Brian, King of Thomond, and again in 1581 by John Fitzgerald of Desmond. A large town that grew around the priory was destroyed in both raids. Dissolved in 1537 and given to the 10th Earl of Ormond.

name: Holy Cross Abbey
description: A magnificently restored Cistercian abbey on the banks of the River Suir — still a living parish church after 800 years, with medieval stonework and a relic of the True Cross.
history: Founded in 1182 by Domnall Mór Ua Briain, King of Thomond, as a Cistercian monastery. Named for a relic of the True Cross brought to Ireland by Plantagenet ancestors. Became a major medieval pilgrimage site. Extensively renovated in the 15th century with exquisite stonework. Fell into ruin after dissolution but was remarkably restored in the 1970s and returned to use as a parish church.

name: Coolbanagher Castle
description: A quiet tower house ruin in the Laois countryside, typical of the fortified residences that once lined the frontier between the English Pale and Gaelic Ireland.
history: Coolbanagher Castle in County Laois is a medieval tower house dating to the 15th or 16th century. The area was on the contested frontier between English-controlled territory and the Gaelic lands of the O'Mores. The nearby Church of Ireland church was designed by the famous architect James Gandon, best known for Dublin's Custom House.

name: Grantstown Castle
description: A tower house ruin in the Tipperary countryside near the River Suir, part of the dense network of Butler strongholds in the Golden Vale.
history: Grantstown Castle is a medieval tower house in County Tipperary. Built in the 15th century, it was part of the extensive Butler domain in the fertile Golden Vale. The tower stands in agricultural land near the River Suir and is representative of the many fortified residences built by Butler vassals and allies.

name: Knockgraffon Castle
description: A medieval motte and castle remains overlooking the Golden Vale, where the ancient coronation mound of the Kings of Munster still rises from the Tipperary fields.
history: Knockgraffon in County Tipperary features a Norman motte built on or near the ancient inauguration site of the Kings of Munster. The motte and castle date to the 12th-13th century period of Norman expansion. The site's dual heritage—pre-Christian royal inauguration mound and Norman military fortification—makes it historically significant.

name: Two-Mile-Borris Castle
description: A tower house near the Tipperary town of Two-Mile-Borris, another link in the chain of medieval fortifications across the Golden Vale.
history: Two-Mile-Borris Castle is a medieval tower house in County Tipperary. The castle was part of the dense network of fortified residences in the rich agricultural lands of the Golden Vale. The area was contested between various Anglo-Norman and Gaelic families throughout the medieval period.

name: Moycarkey Castle
description: A substantial medieval castle ruin in the heart of Tipperary's Golden Vale, once a stronghold of the powerful Butler dynasty.
history: Moycarkey Castle in County Tipperary was a significant Butler stronghold in the Golden Vale. The castle dates to the medieval period and controlled productive agricultural land. The Butlers of Moycarkey were a prominent branch of the wider Butler family that dominated Tipperary for centuries.

name: Dromineer Castle
description: A lakeside castle ruin on the shores of Lough Derg, now a picturesque harbour village popular with sailing enthusiasts.
history: Dromineer Castle stands on the shores of Lough Derg in north Tipperary. The medieval castle guarded the harbour on one of the Shannon's largest lakes. Dromineer is now a popular sailing centre and the castle ruins add character to the pretty waterfront. The village hosts regular sailing regattas.

name: Pottlerath Castle
description: A tower house in the Kilkenny countryside, its walls standing proud among the rolling fields of Ireland's medieval heartland.
history: Pottlerath Castle is a medieval tower house in County Kilkenny. Built in the 15th century, it was one of many such fortified residences in the Butler heartlands of Kilkenny. The tower retains structural integrity and stands in the pastoral landscape typical of the Nore valley region.

name: Kiltinan Castle
description: A massive and imposing Butler fortress near Fethard, one of the largest and most dramatic medieval castles in County Tipperary.
history: Kiltinan Castle near Fethard in County Tipperary is a large medieval castle associated with the Butler family. The impressive fortress features substantial walls and towers and controlled the rich farmland of south Tipperary. It has been partially restored and is in private ownership.

name: Sopwell Castle
description: A quiet ruin near Cloughjordan in north Tipperary, in the historically rich borderlands between Butler and O'Brien territories.
history: Sopwell Castle is a medieval fortification near Cloughjordan in north Tipperary. The area was on the contested border between the Butler lands to the south and the O'Brien territory of Thomond to the north. The castle reflects the military tensions of this frontier zone.

name: Kilbeggan Castle
description: Medieval remains in the Westmeath town of Kilbeggan, home to the oldest licensed distillery in Ireland where whiskey has been made since 1757.
history: Kilbeggan Castle is a medieval fortification in County Westmeath. The town is most famous for Kilbeggan Distillery, established in 1757 and the oldest licensed distillery in Ireland. The castle dates to the medieval period and reflects the town's long history as a settlement on the route between Dublin and the west.

name: Castletown Geoghegan
description: Castle remains in a small Westmeath village bearing the name of the Gaelic MacGeoghegan clan who ruled this territory for centuries.
history: Castletown Geoghegan in County Westmeath takes its name from the MacGeoghegan clan who were lords of this territory in the Irish midlands. The castle dates to the medieval period and served as a MacGeoghegan stronghold. The clan produced notable poets, historians, and soldiers over the centuries.

name: Clonyn Castle
description: A Victorian castellated mansion near Delvin, Westmeath, combining medieval tower house origins with 19th-century Gothic Revival grandeur.
history: Clonyn Castle near Delvin, County Westmeath, evolved from a medieval tower house into a substantial Victorian castellated mansion. The estate was associated with the Greville-Nugent family. The castle combines medieval and Victorian elements in its architecture. The building has been used for various purposes over the years.

name: Multyfarnham Friary
description: A beautifully maintained Franciscan friary in the Westmeath countryside, remarkably still in active religious use after nearly 600 years.
history: Multyfarnham Friary in County Westmeath was founded in 1268 and has been in continuous Franciscan use for centuries, making it one of the longest continuously inhabited religious houses in Ireland. The friary features a fine medieval church and well-maintained grounds with Stations of the Cross set in a life-size Calvary scene.

name: Caulfield Castle
description: Modest castle remains in the Westmeath countryside, associated with the planter families who transformed the midlands in the 17th century.
history: Caulfield Castle in County Westmeath is associated with the Caulfield family, who were granted lands during the plantation period. The castle dates to the 17th century and reflects the transformation of landownership in the Irish midlands during this period.

name: Tyrellspass Castle
description: A picturesque tower house in one of Ireland's prettiest planned villages, where a crescent of Georgian houses curves around a village green.
history: Tyrellspass Castle in County Westmeath stands in one of Ireland's most attractive planned estate villages. The castle was built by the Tyrrell family in the 15th century and later passed to the Belvedere family. The village's crescent of Georgian houses around a green was planned by the Earls of Belvedere, creating an unusually elegant midlands townscape.

name: Rattin Castle
description: A medieval tower in the Westmeath countryside, part of the patchwork of Gaelic and Anglo-Norman fortifications across the midlands.
history: Rattin Castle is a medieval tower house in County Westmeath. The castle dates to the medieval period and was part of the network of fortified residences in the midlands. The area was contested between Gaelic and Anglo-Norman families throughout the medieval period.

name: Moydrum Castle
description: A grand but roofless Victorian Gothic ruin near Athlone, its dramatic shell standing amid parkland in the Westmeath countryside.
history: Moydrum Castle near Athlone was the seat of the Handcock family, Lords Castlemaine. The Gothic Revival castle was burned during the Civil War in 1921 and stands as an impressive shell. The ruin's scale and decorative details, including fine window tracery, make it one of the more striking burned-out Big Houses of the midlands.

name: Castle Forbes
description: The ancestral seat of the Forbes family in County Longford, a grand Georgian house surrounded by extensive parkland.
history: Castle Forbes near Newtownforbes, County Longford, is the seat of the Forbes family, Earls of Granard. The Georgian mansion is set in extensive parkland. The Forbes family has been associated with this area since the plantation period. The estate remains in private hands and is not generally open to the public.

name: Abbeyshrule Abbey
description: Cistercian abbey ruins beside the Royal Canal in the quiet Longford countryside, where medieval monks lived in this watery, pastoral landscape.
history: Abbeyshrule Abbey in County Longford was a Cistercian foundation established in 1200. The abbey ruins stand beside the Royal Canal. The site includes the remains of a church, cloister, and domestic buildings. Abbeyshrule is also home to a small airfield, one of Ireland's oldest, established in 1955.

name: Ardagh Castle
description: Heritage remains in the Longford village of Ardagh, where the famous Ardagh Chalice—Ireland's greatest metalwork treasure—was discovered.
history: Ardagh in County Longford is most famous as the find spot of the Ardagh Chalice, discovered in 1868 and now one of the finest examples of Celtic metalwork in the National Museum of Ireland. The village has medieval castle remains and retains a quiet, heritage-rich character.

name: Edgeworthstown Castle
description: Heritage remains in the town associated with Maria Edgeworth, the pioneering novelist whose work anticipated Jane Austen and influenced Walter Scott.
history: Edgeworthstown (now officially Mostrim) in County Longford was the home of the Edgeworth family. Maria Edgeworth (1768-1849) was a pioneering novelist whose 'Castle Rackrent' (1800) was one of the first regional novels in English. Her father Richard Lovell Edgeworth was a noted inventor and educationalist. The family home has been restored as a heritage centre.

name: Shinrone Castle
description: Castle remains in the Offaly village of Shinrone, in the flat bogland of the Irish midlands where ancient trackways cross the peat.
history: Shinrone Castle in County Offaly dates to the medieval period. The village sits in the midland boglands of Offaly, an area rich in archaeological discoveries preserved in the peat. The castle was associated with local families in the border zone between Munster and Leinster.

name: Ballyboy Castle
description: A medieval tower house near the Offaly village of Ballyboy, in the rolling hills between the boglands and the Slieve Bloom Mountains.
history: Ballyboy Castle in County Offaly is a medieval tower house near the foothills of the Slieve Bloom Mountains. The castle dates to the 15th or 16th century and was associated with local families in this border area between the Irish midlands and the uplands of the Slieve Blooms.

name: Seir Kieran Monastery
description: One of Ireland's oldest monastic sites, founded before St Patrick arrived, where ancient crosses and gravestones span 1,500 years of Christian worship.
history: Seir Kieran near Clareen in County Offaly is one of Ireland's oldest monastic sites, traditionally founded by St Ciaran of Saigir in the 5th century—possibly before St Patrick's mission. The site preserves early cross slabs, high cross fragments, and medieval church ruins. It remains an active graveyard, creating an unbroken link to the earliest days of Irish Christianity.

name: Durrow Abbey
description: The monastery where the magnificent Book of Durrow was created—one of Ireland's earliest illuminated manuscripts and a masterwork of Celtic art.
history: Durrow Abbey in County Offaly was founded by St Columcille in the 6th century. The monastery produced the Book of Durrow (c.650-700 AD), one of the earliest and most beautiful illuminated gospel books, now in Trinity College Dublin. The site preserves a fine 10th-century high cross and the remains of the medieval church. The surrounding parkland was later part of a country estate.

name: Lemenaghan Castle
description: Medieval remains near an ancient monastic site in the Offaly boglands, where the Shannon Callows create one of Ireland's most important wetland habitats.
history: Lemenaghan Castle in County Offaly stands near the ancient monastery of Lemanaghan, associated with St Manchan. The area is on the edge of the Shannon Callows, extensive seasonal wetlands along the Shannon that support rare wildlife. The medieval castle and nearby monastic remains create a rich heritage landscape.

name: Emo Court
description: A magnificent neoclassical mansion designed by James Gandon, set in sweeping parkland and gardens—one of Ireland's finest architectural treasures.
history: Emo Court in County Laois was designed in 1790 by James Gandon (architect of Dublin's Custom House) for the 1st Earl of Portarlington. The neoclassical house features a magnificent rotunda and is surrounded by beautiful parkland with a lake and woodland walks. Restored by Jesuits and later the state, it is now managed by the OPW and open to visitors.

name: Timahoe Round Tower
description: One of Ireland's finest round towers, its remarkable Romanesque doorway featuring human faces and interlaced patterns of extraordinary beauty.
history: The Round Tower at Timahoe, County Laois, dates to the 12th century and stands 29 metres tall. Its most remarkable feature is the ornate Romanesque doorway at first-floor level, decorated with human faces, animal heads, and interlaced patterns. The tower is associated with a monastery founded by St Mochua in the 6th century. It is considered one of the finest round towers in Ireland.

name: Killeshin Church
description: A Romanesque church doorway of extraordinary richness in the Laois countryside—one of Ireland's finest examples of 12th-century carved stonework.
history: Killeshin Church in County Laois preserves a magnificent Romanesque doorway dating to the 12th century. The doorway features multiple orders of richly carved chevron, interlace, and figural decoration. The church was part of a monastery founded by St Comgan. The doorway is considered one of the finest examples of Irish Romanesque architecture.

name: Shaen Castle
description: Gothic Revival castle ruins in the Laois countryside, a dramatic Victorian shell surrounded by woodland and parkland.
history: Shaen Castle in County Laois was a Gothic Revival mansion built by the Fitzgerald family. The castle was destroyed by fire and stands as an impressive ruin in woodland parkland. The estate contains fine mature trees and is accessible via forest walks.

name: Heywood Gardens
description: A hidden gem of formal garden design by Sir Edwin Lutyens, featuring terraces, lakes, and plantings in the gentle Laois landscape.
history: Heywood Gardens near Ballinakill, County Laois, were designed by the great architect Sir Edwin Lutyens with planting by Gertrude Jekyll around 1909. The formal gardens feature terraces, a sunken garden, pergola, and ornamental lake. Though the house was demolished, the gardens have been restored by the OPW and are freely open to visitors.

name: Kilcash Castle
description: A magnificent Butler tower house at the foot of Slievenamon, immortalised in the haunting Irish lament 'Cill Chais'—one of the great poems in the Irish language.
history: Kilcash Castle in County Tipperary was a principal seat of the Butler family beneath Slievenamon mountain. The castle dates to the 15th century. It is the subject of the famous Irish poem 'Cill Chais' ('Kilcash'), lamenting the decay of the Gaelic world: 'What shall we do for timber? The last of the woods is down.' The evocative ruins stand in a sublime setting.

name: Farney Castle
description: A restored medieval tower house in County Tipperary, its round corners and fine stonework making it a distinctive landmark in the Golden Vale.
history: Farney Castle in County Tipperary is a medieval tower house with distinctive round corner towers. The castle has been restored and is in private ownership. Its unusual architectural features, including the round turrets, distinguish it from the many rectangular tower houses in the region.

name: Portlick Castle
description: A medieval tower house on the shores of Lough Ree in County Westmeath, overlooking one of the Shannon's largest and most beautiful lakes.
history: Portlick Castle on the shores of Lough Ree, County Westmeath, is a medieval tower house dating to the 15th century. The castle was held by the Dillon family and later by the Nugent family. Its lakeside position on Lough Ree provides fine views across the water. The castle has been restored in recent years.

name: Killua Castle
description: A magnificent Gothic Revival castle in the Westmeath countryside, recently restored after decades of dereliction into a stunning cultural venue.
history: Killua Castle near Clonmellon, County Westmeath, was the seat of the Chapman family (ancestors of T.E. Lawrence, 'Lawrence of Arabia'). The castle dates to the 15th century with substantial Gothic Revival additions. After decades of abandonment, it has been spectacularly restored as a luxury event venue.

name: Greville Arms Tower
description: A historic tower in the Westmeath town of Mullingar, associated with the Greville family and the hotel that has hosted Irish literary figures.
history: The Greville Arms in Mullingar is a historic hotel with medieval tower origins. The hotel has hosted notable literary figures including James Joyce, who spent time in the town. Mullingar is the county town of Westmeath and a major midlands centre.

name: Dun na Si Heritage Park
description: A heritage park in Moate, County Westmeath, recreating aspects of Irish rural life and history across the centuries.
history: Dun na Si Heritage Park in Moate, County Westmeath, is a heritage centre and park that explores Irish rural life, folklore, and history. The park features a reconstructed crannog, famine cottage, and other historical structures. It offers an accessible introduction to Irish heritage for families and visitors.

name: Ballymore Castle (Westmeath)
description: Substantial Norman castle remains in the Westmeath village of Ballymore, where massive earthworks testify to the scale of medieval fortification.
history: Ballymore Castle in County Westmeath is a substantial Norman castle with impressive earthwork defences. The castle controlled the route through the midlands and was an important Anglo-Norman stronghold. The large motte and extensive earthworks indicate the site's medieval military significance.

name: Corlea Trackway
description: A magnificently preserved Iron Age timber road across the Longford boglands, built in 148 BC and now displayed in a purpose-built visitor centre.
history: The Corlea Trackway near Kenagh, County Longford, is a large Iron Age road of oak planks built across a bog in 148 BC. Discovered in the 1980s, it is the largest prehistoric trackway found in Europe. The remarkable timbers are preserved in a purpose-built visitor centre that maintains the controlled atmosphere needed to prevent the ancient wood from deteriorating.

name: Dungar Castle
description: A tower house in the Waterford countryside near Roscrea, part of the medieval defensive landscape of the southeast.
history: Dungar Castle is a medieval tower house. The castle dates to the medieval period and was part of the network of fortified residences in the region. The tower stands in agricultural land.

name: Kinnitty Castle
description: A Gothic Revival castle at the foot of the Slieve Bloom Mountains where Norman invaders, Gaelic chieftains, and ghosts of monks have left centuries of turbulent Irish history.
history: The first castle was destroyed in 1209 during the Norman invasion and rebuilt in 1213. An Augustinian abbey (St Finnian's) was established nearby, leaving a High Cross that still survives. Later held by the O'Carroll clan of Éile. The current Gothic Revival castle dates from the 19th century, built on the medieval foundations. Now operates as a hotel, reputedly one of Ireland's most haunted.

name: Castlegrace
description: A tower house in the south Tipperary countryside, one of many Butler strongholds in the productive agricultural heartland of the Golden Vale.
history: Castlegrace is a medieval tower house in County Tipperary. The castle was associated with the Grace family (from whom it takes its name) and the wider Butler lordship in the Golden Vale. The tower dates to the medieval period.

name: Kilcooly Abbey
description: A magnificently preserved Cistercian abbey in County Tipperary, featuring some of the finest medieval stone carvings in Ireland.
history: Kilcooly Abbey near Urlingford, County Tipperary, is a Cistercian monastery founded in 1184. After being damaged in the 15th century, it was rebuilt with extraordinary stone carvings including a remarkable sacristy doorway and a unique cloister arcade with figural sculptures. The quality of the stone carving rivals any medieval site in Ireland.

name: Abbeyleix Heritage House
description: A heritage centre in one of Ireland's most attractive planned towns, where the de Vesci family created an elegant estate village.
history: Abbeyleix Heritage House in County Laois tells the story of this beautiful planned estate town created by the de Vesci family. The town is considered one of Ireland's most attractive, with its wide main street and fine buildings. The heritage centre, in the former North Boys School, also covers the Cistercian abbey founded here in 1183.

name: Kilbeggan Abbey
description: Medieval monastic remains near the distillery town of Kilbeggan in Westmeath, adding ecclesiastical heritage to the town's whiskey story.
history: Kilbeggan Abbey is a medieval monastic site near Kilbeggan, County Westmeath. The town is primarily known for its distillery (established 1757), but the abbey remains add a medieval dimension to the local heritage. The monastic site dates to the early Christian period.

name: Longford Castle
description: Castle remains in the county town of Longford, where the O'Farrells once held sway over the Irish midlands.
history: Longford Castle stood in the county town of Longford and was associated with the O'Farrell clan and later English planter families. The town developed as a market centre and the castle served various administrative functions. Little of the original medieval castle survives in the modern town.

name: Fethard Castle
description: Medieval town walls and castle in one of Ireland's best-preserved medieval towns, where an almost complete circuit of walls still stands in County Tipperary.
history: Fethard in County Tipperary is one of the best-preserved medieval walled towns in Ireland. The town retains an almost complete circuit of medieval walls, several tower houses, a medieval main street, and a fine Augustinian priory. The castle and town walls together create one of Ireland's most intact medieval urban landscapes.

name: Clonmel West Gate
description: The only surviving gate of Clonmel's medieval town walls, in the largest town in County Tipperary and a centre of commerce since Norman times.
history: The West Gate is the only surviving gate of Clonmel's medieval town walls. Clonmel is the largest town in County Tipperary and has a rich medieval heritage. The town walls were substantial enough for the garrison to resist Oliver Cromwell's forces for several weeks in 1650—one of the few towns to offer serious resistance.

name: Monaincha Abbey
description: A remarkable island monastery in a drained bog near Roscrea, where a Romanesque church and high cross stand in extraordinary isolation.
history: Monaincha Abbey near Roscrea, County Tipperary, was originally on an island in a bog lake (now drained). The site preserves a fine 12th-century Romanesque church and a high cross. The extraordinary setting—a stone church apparently standing in the middle of flat bogland—creates one of Ireland's most unusual heritage scenes.

name: Castletown Castle Laois
description: Castle remains in the Laois midlands, part of the complex history of plantation and resistance in Ireland's central counties.
history: Castletown Castle in County Laois dates to the medieval/plantation period. The area was at the heart of the Tudor plantation of Laois (Queen's County) in the 16th century, one of the earliest and most contested colonial ventures in Ireland.

name: Coolbanagher Church
description: An elegant 18th-century Church of Ireland church in Laois designed by the great James Gandon, architect of Dublin's Custom House.
history: Coolbanagher Church in County Laois was designed by James Gandon in 1785, the architect of Dublin's Custom House and Four Courts. The elegant church is considered one of Gandon's lesser-known masterpieces and features fine Classical proportions. It stands in the quiet Laois countryside.

name: Castle Durrow
description: A magnificent Palladian mansion in County Laois, now a luxury castle hotel set in parkland designed by Capability Brown.
history: Castle Durrow in County Laois was built in 1716 for Colonel William Flower, 1st Viscount Ashbrook. The Palladian mansion is set in grounds attributed to Capability Brown. The building has been restored as a luxury castle hotel. The nearby town of Durrow hosts Ireland's annual Scarecrow Festival.

name: Ballycullen Castle
description: A tower house in the Tipperary countryside, part of the extensive network of medieval fortifications across the Golden Vale.
history: Ballycullen Castle is a medieval tower house in County Tipperary. The castle dates to the 15th century and was one of the many tower houses in the productive agricultural lands of the Golden Vale.

name: Killaleigh Church
description: A medieval church ruin in the Offaly countryside, preserving architectural heritage amid the quiet midland landscape.
history: Killaleigh Church is a medieval church ruin in County Offaly. The church dates to the medieval period and stands in the quiet Offaly countryside.

name: Shannonbridge Fortification
description: A massive Napoleonic-era artillery fortification guarding a strategic Shannon crossing, one of the most impressive military heritage sites in the midlands.
history: The Shannonbridge Fortification in County Offaly is a substantial early 19th-century military installation built to defend a strategic crossing of the River Shannon during the Napoleonic Wars. The massive fort features thick walls, gun emplacements, and defensive positions. It is one of the largest Napoleonic-era fortifications in Ireland.

name: Thurles Castle
description: A medieval tower in the heart of Tipperary's cathedral town, where the GAA was founded in 1884, transforming Irish sport and culture.
history: Thurles Castle (also known as Bridge Castle) stands in the town where the Gaelic Athletic Association (GAA) was founded at Hayes's Hotel on 1 November 1884—one of the most significant cultural events in modern Irish history. The castle dates to the medieval period.

name: Burncourt Castle
description: The spectacular roofless shell of an enormous fortified house in County Tipperary, deliberately burned by its owner to deny it to Cromwell's forces.
history: Burncourt Castle (Clogheen) in County Tipperary was built by Sir Richard Everard around 1641. The massive mansion—with 26 gables and seven chimneys—was burned by Lady Everard to prevent it falling to Cromwellian forces. The impressive shell, one of the largest roofless ruins in Ireland, stands in farmland in the Galtee foothills.

name: Moorstown Castle
description: A well-preserved fortified house in the Tipperary countryside, displaying the transition from medieval castle to Tudor mansion.
history: Moorstown Castle in County Tipperary is a well-preserved fortified house dating to the early 17th century. The building displays the transition from medieval tower house to more comfortable domestic architecture. It features fine window details and retains good structural integrity.

name: Carrick-on-Suir Castle
description: See Ormond Castle—the finest Elizabethan manor house in Ireland, built by Black Tom Butler to welcome Queen Elizabeth I.
history: Carrick-on-Suir's Ormond Castle is the finest surviving Elizabethan manor in Ireland, built by the 10th Earl of Ormond. See Ormond Castle entry for full details.

name: Lackan Castle
description: A tower house in the Westmeath midlands, part of the medieval fortified landscape of the Irish central plain.
history: Lackan Castle is a medieval tower house in County Westmeath.

name: Tristernagh Abbey
description: Augustinian priory ruins near the shores of Lough Iron in Westmeath, in the quiet lake country of the Irish midlands.
history: Tristernagh Abbey near Lough Iron, County Westmeath, was an Augustinian priory. The ruins stand near the lake in the quiet Westmeath midlands.

name: Ballyfin Demesne
description: Ireland's most luxurious country house hotel, a magnificent Regency mansion set in 614 acres of parkland, lakes, and formal gardens.
history: Ballyfin Demesne in County Laois is a grand Regency mansion built in the 1820s for Sir Charles Coote. The house was designed by Dominick Madden and Richard Morrison and features extraordinary interiors. After years as a school, it was restored and reopened in 2011 as Ireland's most exclusive country house hotel, set in 614 acres of parkland.

name: Fancroft Mill Castle
description: A tower house near Roscrea in County Tipperary, with an associated mill that reflects the agricultural heritage of the area.
history: Fancroft Mill Castle near Roscrea, County Tipperary, combines a medieval tower house with a mill, reflecting the agricultural and commercial heritage of the area.

name: Killenaule Castle
description: A tower in the south Tipperary town of Killenaule, in the productive agricultural heartland of the Golden Vale.
history: Killenaule Castle is a medieval tower in County Tipperary.

name: Castle Otway
description: A castellated mansion in the Tipperary countryside near Templemore, surrounded by parkland in the shadow of the Devil's Bit mountain.
history: Castle Otway in County Tipperary is a castellated mansion near Templemore. The estate is set in parkland near the Devil's Bit mountain.

name: Newcastle Tipperary Castle
description: Castle remains in the Tipperary village of Newcastle, in the foothills of the Galtee Mountains.
history: Newcastle Castle in County Tipperary stands in a village in the foothills of the Galtee Mountains, Ireland's highest inland range.

name: Ballynaclogh Castle
description: A tower house in north Tipperary, in the area between the Devil's Bit and Silvermines mountains.
history: Ballynaclogh Castle is a medieval tower house in County Tipperary.

name: Lorrha Priory
description: Augustinian priory and Dominican friary ruins in the heritage village of Lorrha, north Tipperary, one of Ireland's most important medieval ecclesiastical sites.
history: Lorrha in north Tipperary preserves the ruins of both an Augustinian priory and a Dominican friary, making it one of the most significant medieval ecclesiastical sites in Munster. The monastery has origins in the 6th century and produced the Stowe Missal, an important early Irish manuscript.

name: Fennor Castle
description: A tower house in the Meath countryside.
history: Fennor Castle is a medieval tower house in County Meath.

name: Any Castle
description: A small tower in the Limerick countryside.
history: Any Castle is a medieval fortification in County Limerick.

name: Ballyloughloe Castle
description: A tower house in the Westmeath countryside near the River Shannon.
history: Ballyloughloe Castle is a medieval tower house in County Westmeath.

name: Kilcormac Castle
description: Castle remains in the Offaly town of Kilcormac, in the heart of the Irish midlands.
history: Kilcormac Castle is a medieval fortification in County Offaly.

name: Banagher Castle
description: Castle remains in the charming Shannon-side town of Banagher, where Anthony Trollope began his career and Charlotte Bronte spent her honeymoon.
history: Banagher in County Offaly has castle remains on the Shannon. The town is notable as the place where Anthony Trollope began his writing career and where Charlotte Bronte spent part of her honeymoon in 1854. Banagher's fine stone bridge spans the Shannon.

name: Aghancon Castle
description: A tower house in the Offaly countryside, near the Slieve Bloom Mountains.
history: Aghancon Castle is a medieval tower house in County Offaly near the Slieve Bloom Mountains.
